framework_summary:
The 4R Framework categorizes DTs into four increasing levels of capability that allow organizations to plan DT development according to their desired maturity and functional scope, aligning technical effort with business objectives. This framework was designed to create a conceptual model that defines and guides the development of the DTs through distinct phases.
The 4R framework categorizes DTs into four phases: Representation (R1), Replication (R2), Reality (R3), and Relational (R4), each representing increasing levels of maturity and capability. The R1 phase involves understanding the physical system's behavior and setting up a data architecture and storage system. The goal is to define the features, behaviors, components, and relationships relevant for the use case, and identify what to include in the model and what not to. In the R2 phase, the system is replicated in a virtual environment using the data architecture established in the representation phase, reproducing the same outputs given the same inputs from the physical system. It is necessary that this phase provides accurate mapping of inputs as it is fed the data from R1 to ultimately become the replica used to model the physical system. The R3 phase involves using the DT to explore what-if scenarios, with the aim of implementing the results obtained from virtual runs into the physical system. This creates a virtual modeling tool that can now run independently of the physical system with the ability to make predictions, provide alternative scenarios, and optimizations. The R4 phase involves incorporating decision-making technologies such as AI or ML and enabling bi-directional data exchange. This allows the model to translate updates, corrections, and decisions back into the physical system, making the twin self-aware.
Each level of the 4R Framework builds upon the previous one, progressively increasing the model’s analytical depth and decision-making capability. As the capability advances from R1 to R4, the DT goes from the ground level of data gathering and identifying useful components to full autonomy. This hierarchical structure demonstrates the progression from analyzing and applying data to build a digital replica, training that replica to optimize the physical system, and ultimately enabling the DT to make independent updates and corrections to the physical system, which form the foundation requirements of DT implementation. By clearly defining these stages, the 4R Framework enables researchers and practitioners to assess their desired DT capabilities, identify existing gaps, and determine the necessary level of support needed for their engineering objectives. Ultimately, the 4R framework establishes a systematic approach for transitioning from data architecture development to the creation of an intelligent, data-driven DT capable of continuous feedback, optimization, and real-time decision support.

assessment_rules.current_level_rule: The current 4R level is the highest consecutive level whose minimum gate criteria are sufficiently supported by explicit evidence.

recommended_scoring.pass_rule: A level passes when every minimum gate criterion scores at least 1 and at least 80% of the criteria score 2.
recommended_scoring.fallback_rule: If a higher level has some evidence but a lower level is not fully satisfied, assign the lower unmet level as current.

assessment_rules.action_item_rule: Recommend action items by taking the unmet minimum gate criteria of the next desired level and converting them into concrete tasks, starting with foundational gaps first.
assessment_rules.sequence_rule: Do not recommend R3 or R4 actions as primary next steps when major R1 or R2 gaps remain.

levels.name: Representation
levels.description: In the Representation (R1) phase is, it is necessary to understand the physical system, decide on the significant features of the system for the use case, and create a system architecture for data collection and data storage from the physical component. In this first level of the framework, the foundational digital representation of the physical system is developed. An understanding of the inner working of the system is required to appropriately represent a system. This phase primarily considers the identification of key properties, characteristics and states that comprise the physical entity. Once the qualities and characteristics have been identified and categorized, the design of the processes to collect data that are both directly and indirectly associated with each property commences. This is the phase where decisions regarding the purpose of DT should be made since the purpose of the DT is critical to the development of the terminal DT phase and the implementation of the Representation is a critical component. A high-level DT implementation would likely not require high resolution data whereas a more complex DT will. Some characteristics of the representation phase include the physical system represented as data, the data flows from the physical device to the virtual device, and the data to be collected, stored, and verified. The data collected at this phase allows for understanding of the system and is used for analysis, visualization, and model creation. Considerations in this phase are: (i) Data collection pipeline (ii) Data persistence/storage, (iii) Data polling rate (iv) Data structures and format (v) Metadata (vi) information design modeling (vii) Data preprocessing pipelines (viii) Data analysis and representation. It is important to highlight that the representation process is dynamic and evolves with the implementation of the next phases of the framework. As requirements for the DT change over time, so does the data needed to support these capabilities. Representation should be designed in a modular and scalable way, so that new requirements are served in a plug and play manner, with minimal effect to the rest of the system.
minimum_gate_criteria: The digital twin purpose, scope, and supported decisions are explicitly defined.
System boundaries, major components, inputs/outputs, and important states are identified.
Critical variables are selected without major over-instrumentation or missing context.
A repeatable data collection and storage pipeline exists.
Collected data is structured, usable, and verified against known system behavior.
diagnostic_checks: Can the system be explained using only the data being collected?
Is each data stream tied to a system component, state, or decision?
Is the data clean, structured, and ready for analysis or modeling?
Does the data pipeline run reliably without manual intervention?
Does the level of detail match the intended use case?
action_items_to_reach_this_level: Write a one-paragraph statement defining the DT purpose, target decisions, and expected outcomes.
Document system boundaries, included/excluded components, and key interactions.
List static properties and dynamic states, then identify the minimum critical variables required.
Select sensors, APIs, or software sources and define the acquisition method for each.
Choose storage and data formats, then create naming conventions and metadata fields.
Implement preprocessing and data quality checks so the data is analysis-ready.
Validate collected data against known outputs or trusted reference readings.
levels.target_if_not_met: Reach R1 before attempting R2 work.

levels.name: Replication
levels.description:
In the Replication (R2) phase, will utilize the architecture and data collected from the initial phase to develop a digital
system in a virtual environment that is a replication of the cyber physical system. At the end of this phase, the DT should have the capability to produce the same results as the cyber physical system given the same inputs.

After representing the system to the necessary detail from the requirements and scope generated in the Representation phase, an accurate Replication of the system can be undertaken. The data and relationships determined from Representation are used to create a digital system in a virtual environment. The digital system replicates the components of the system selected in the Representation phase. One key difference between the Replication and Representation phase is that the virtual device cannot function without the physical device and data does not need to be stored. Some of the important considerations while replicating a system or process are synchronization between the physical
device and the virtual device, developing models, how data flows affect different parts of the model, and validating the models. The first step of the Replication phase is the transition from data to a functional digital entity. This is where CAD software is employed to build a realistic 3D model of the physical system. Data and relationships developed during the Representation stage are utilized to drive the virtual model and provide insight to its operation. There are several challenges to overcome when transitioning from Representation to Replication. While latency is not critical to Representation, it is of high significance to the Replication phase. Real-time decision making must be supported by real-time data acquisition and optimized pre-processing workflows. Yet, real-time is hard to achieve, therefore such systems typically function in a soft-real-time setting. The accurate performance of the Replication DT in the virtual environment achieves verification of the DT.
minimum_gate_criteria: R1 is already satisfied.
Replication targets, required outputs, and acceptable error thresholds are defined.
A modeling platform has been selected and the system has been mapped into a digital architecture.
Live or recorded data can drive model inputs and updates.
The model has been tested against real behavior and verified to reproduce outputs with acceptable deviation.
diagnostic_checks: Can the DT take real inputs and reproduce real outputs?
Can deviations between model and reality be explained?
Can the DT run continuously with synchronized data?
action_items_to_reach_this_level: Define which system outputs must be reproduced and set allowable error thresholds.
Select the modeling environment and justify why it fits the system behavior.
Map each physical component or subsystem to a digital counterpart.
Implement system logic, interfaces, and state behavior in the virtual model.
Connect live or recorded real-world data to the model.
Run controlled comparison scenarios against the physical system.
Calibrate the model until the observed deviation is acceptably low.
levels.target_if_not_met: Close R2 replication gaps before claiming predictive capability.

levels.name: Reality
levels.description:
The third phase, Reality (R3), will allow the DT to work independently of the physical system, and can provide accurate predictions given the same set of inputs into the cyber physical system. The DT can be used to investiage alternative scenarios and the results of the virtual runs can be used tooptimize use of the physical system.

At this phase the DT has the capability of independently providing outcomes given a set of inputs. Given a set of inputs or scenarios, the DT does not simply replicate the physical system, but it can predict the outcome of how the physical system will perform. Machine learning models are continuously trained to improve the prediction. The main characteristics of the Reality phase are the ability to run the virtual device independent of the physical device, run different configurations or use cases and come out with a different version by changing inputs. The eality phase considers the augmentation of the DT with non-essential, but value-added capabilities, such as remote-control options, Extended eality (XR), and optimization methods. Upon successful development of this phase the DT can be used to control the physical system, experiment with parameter variation, and perform optimization study of the process. XR can provide the capability of high-level monitoring and understanding of the physical system’s operation, as well a visual validation. Machine control, in combination with XR allow for the design of plethora of training scenarios and situations. This enables both synchronous and asynchronous remote training for new workers and operators, omething that could dramatically reduce training time and increase efficiency.
minimum_gate_criteria: R2 is already satisfied.
Prediction targets, decision variables, and objective functions are defined.
The DT can run without continuous live dependency on the physical system.
Predictive or scenario-exploration models are implemented.
The DT can evaluate what-if conditions and provide actionable recommendations or optimized results.
diagnostic_checks: Can the DT run independently and still produce credible outcomes?
Can it predict behavior for scenarios not directly observed?
Can it explore what-if alternatives by changing inputs?
Does it produce actionable insights or optimal decisions, not just raw outputs?
Would the team trust it to guide improvement or control decisions?
action_items_to_reach_this_level: Define what the DT must predict, the time horizon, and the decisions it should support.
Decouple the DT from continuous live data so it can run hypothetical cases.
Add predictive, physics-based, machine learning, or analytical models as appropriate.
Implement scenario simulation and what-if analysis workflows.
Compare predictions to known outcomes and quantify predictive performance.
Use iterative tuning or optimization to improve system recommendations.
Create a decision-support output that translates predictions into operational guidance.
levels.target_if_not_met: Complete R3 before pursuing autonomy or closed-loop action.

levels.name: Relational
levels.description:
In the final phase, Relational (R4) , that DT would utilize technologies such as machine learning and artificial intelligence to aid in decision making and can interact bidirectionally with the cyber physical machine to make real time decisions and implement actions. The DT would also have a level of autonomy that is not available in the previous phases.

In the Relational phase, the DT becomes self-aware and is enhanced with the power and problem-solving capabilities of Artificial Intelligence (AI). The DT learns and evolves absorbing experience about the behaviour of the physical asset making the DT operations completely autonomous with the ability to react to anomalies and take necessary corrective action with little or no human interaction. Data flows are utilized to build data sets, aiming to improve certain aspects of the physical entity’s operation. These datasets can subsequently be utilized to train Machine Learning algorithms that would provide the DT with some level of autonomy. Example use cases of such solutions are pre-
dictive maintenance, quality control, and energy consumption forecasting.
minimum_gate_criteria: R3 is already satisfied.
Autonomy scope, human-in-the-loop boundaries, and override conditions are defined.
The DT can detect anomalies or changing conditions in real time.
The DT can choose among actions based on learned or adaptive logic.
Past outcomes are stored and used to improve future decisions.
The DT can make or execute decisions with acceptable trust and safety controls.
diagnostic_checks: Can the DT improve its decisions over time without explicit reprogramming?
Can it detect anomalies and decide what to do automatically?
Can it balance multiple objectives such as cost, quality, and efficiency?
Does it adapt based on past outcomes and experience?
Would stakeholders trust it to make or execute decisions with minimal human intervention?
action_items_to_reach_this_level: Define whether the DT is advisory, semi-autonomous, or fully autonomous.
Specify safety boundaries, override rules, and operator approval requirements.
Integrate control-system connections and real-time anomaly detection.
Implement response strategies such as alerting, adjustment, shutdown, or rerouting.
Add decision logic that compares and selects actions in real time.
Store historical context, decisions, and outcomes to create system memory.
Continuously retrain or update decision logic using new evidence while monitoring trust, safety, and performance.
evidence_expected: Evidence of performance improvement over time
levels.target_if_not_met: Do not claim R4 unless adaptive decision-making and controlled autonomy are both demonstrated.
